us-politics.headline: U.S. Rescues Airman as Iran War Nears Completion, Trump Says
us-politics.body:
The U.S. military rescued a downed airman from Iran, President Trump announced, calling the operation a success. Trump simultaneously escalated threats against Tehran, telling Sunday show audiences the war is "near completion" while warning of consequences if Iran does not reach a deal.

The back-to-back signals - a military rescue and fresh ultimatums - reflect the administration's dual-track approach: projecting strength while leaving a diplomatic door open. Critics question what "near completion" means in practice, and whether the rhetoric risks miscalculation. Supporters argue it demonstrates decisive leadership under pressure.

sources.title: Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran
Sunday shows preview: Trump ups threats on Iran after saying war ‘near completion’

us-politics.headline: Judge Blocks Trump College Admissions Order; DHS Disputes Van Hollen
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked the Trump administration's attempt to require colleges to certify that race played no role in admissions decisions, dealing a setback to its post-affirmative-action enforcement push. The ruling keeps pressure off institutions while legal challenges proceed.

Separately, DHS pushed back hard against Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen, who claimed an illegal immigrant caused a crash while fleeing ICE. DHS says the facts support its account and accused Van Hollen of misrepresenting the incident. The dispute illustrates the sharp partisan divide over immigration enforcement, with each side accusing the other of distorting the record.

sources.title: Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE

us-politics.headline: Wisconsin Supreme Court Race Exposes Republican Divisions
us-politics.body:
Wisconsin's high-profile judicial election is highlighting fractures inside the state Republican Party, with the race drawing national attention as a bellwether for GOP cohesion heading into future cycles. Democrats have used state supreme court contests to consolidate judicial power in recent years.

Republican struggles in Wisconsin mirror broader tensions between the party's establishment and populist wings over candidate selection and messaging. The outcome will shape the court's ideological balance on issues including redistricting and abortion - making it a closely watched test of whether Republicans can unify around judicial candidates in competitive states.

sources.title: Wisconsin judicial race spotlights state GOP’s struggles

us-politics.headline: Vehicle Hits Louisiana Parade Crowd; California Wildfire Contained
us-politics.body:
Several people were injured after a vehicle drove into a crowd at a Louisiana parade, adding to a recent string of vehicle-ramming incidents at public gatherings. Authorities are investigating the circumstances and motive.

On the West Coast, firefighting crews made significant progress containing a Southern California wildfire, though mandatory evacuation orders remain in place for some areas. Residents in affected zones are waiting for clearance to return home. Both incidents underscore ongoing public safety pressures facing local and state authorities.

sources.title: Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade
Crews making progress containing Southern California wildfire as some mandatory evacuations remain

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that normal fuel supplies will continue, as Prime Minister Albanese works to head off a domestic shortage. The reassurances follow a period of acute pressure on petrol prices, with nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices. Motorists in NSW are now being urged to report suspected price gouging through the FuelCheck platform, which has received a funding boost.

Western Australia is expanding its FuelWatch scheme, requiring all regional stations to report prices from May. Farmers are among those hit hardest, with some saying rising fuel costs make it difficult to cover basic expenses. The government's response spans price transparency enforcement, diplomatic assurances, and regulatory expansion.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands
Motorists urged to dob in service stations over fuel price hikes

au-politics.headline: Murder arrest at Melbourne airport; police shooting in Tasmania
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport while attempting to board an international flight, and has since been charged with murdering a woman. Police did not release details on the victim or the accused's intended destination.

In a separate incident, a man is in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police in Goodwood, southern Tasmania. Circumstances surrounding both events remain under investigation. Meanwhile, the sisters of Victoria Police officer Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, have announced plans to sue Victoria Police, alleging the force abandoned them in the aftermath of his death.

sources.title: Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police
'Abandoned': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police

au-politics.headline: Easter long weekend: crashes, sport, and celebrations across Australia
au-politics.body:
A seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east over the Easter weekend, one of several fatal crashes recorded across the country. The North West Coastal Highway reopened after Cyclone Narelle damage, giving Easter travellers long-awaited access to regional Western Australia.

On the sporting front, Tim Tszyu claimed a boxing title in Wollongong, the Sydney Kings broke the NBL trophy after a dramatic overtime win over the Adelaide 36ers, Melbourne defeated GWS at the MCG, and the Newcastle Knights beat the Canberra Raiders in the NRL. Australians broadly marked Easter with community events, despite a backdrop of global economic uncertainty.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Tim Tszyu dominates Denis Nurja to claim title in Wollongong
Australians find joy amid global uncertainty in Easter celebrations
Live: Demons down the Suns at MCG ahead of Bulldogs vs Bombers
Live: Raiders fall to defeat once more as Knights charge on in Newcastle
Kings break NBL trophy after dramatic overtime win as 36ers blow late lead
Travellers celebrate as major highway reopens after Cyclone Narelle
Walsh facing up to six weeks on sidelines with fractured cheekbone

au-politics.headline: US airman rescued from Iran as diplomatic signals emerge
au-politics.body:
A US F-15 airman was rescued from Iran following a special forces operation, after his aircraft came down in Iranian territory. The rescue adds complexity to an already tense diplomatic picture. Separately, Iran has signalled a potential opening toward the Trump administration, with analysts describing the move as a calculated olive branch rather than a substantive policy shift.

The two developments together suggest a cautious, back-channel dynamic between Washington and Tehran. Neither government has made formal statements confirming direct negotiations.

sources.title: Iran has offered Trump an olive branch
US F-15 airman rescued in Iran after special forces operation

world.headline: US Rescues Second Pilot as Iran War Enters Week Six
world.body:
Trump confirmed the rescue of a second downed US airman from Iran on day 37 of the US-Israeli military campaign, calling it "miraculous." The pilot's F-15 was shot down over Iranian territory, and a covert operation retrieved him before Iranian forces could secure his location. Tehran has rejected US ultimatums and shows no sign of backing down.

The war is generating significant domestic opposition, with polling showing the conflict broadly unpopular among Americans. Meanwhile, US satellite firm Planet Labs announced a blackout on imagery of the conflict zone, limiting independent visibility of strikes and damage. The US also arrested relatives of slain Iranian general Qasem Soleimani, a move likely intended as additional leverage on Tehran.

sources.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images
Iran war live: Trump confirms rescue of US officer, says ‘we got him’
US says it has arrested relatives of late Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani

world.headline: Houthis Strike Ben Gurion; Hormuz Tensions Simmer
world.body:
Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile attack on Israel's Ben Gurion airport, escalating their involvement in the broader regional conflict. The strike signals that the US-Israeli campaign against Iran has continued to draw in allied armed groups across the Middle East.

Iran separately announced that Iraqi ships may transit the Strait of Hormuz freely, a selective carve-out that underscores Tehran's use of the waterway as a pressure point. Transits through the strait have ticked upward despite the conflict, but Iran's ability to restrict or permit passage remains a live economic threat. Pakistan is also navigating serious fallout, caught between its proximity to Iran and pressure from Western allies.

sources.title: Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport
Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
How will Pakistan deal with the fallout from Iran war?

world.headline: Holy Week Under Occupation: Palestinian Christians Face Tight Restrictions
world.body:
Palestinian Christians marked a subdued Holy Week this year as Israeli restrictions sharply curtailed movement and access to religious sites. In Hebron, Israeli measures have tightened control over the Ibrahimi Mosque, limiting Muslim worshippers during Ramadan while expanding Jewish access - a pattern critics say is accelerating the erasure of Palestinian religious life in the city.

Indonesia received the bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon, a reminder that the regional conflict continues to claim lives beyond its immediate borders. The combination of restricted worship, contested holy sites, and ongoing military violence is reshaping daily life across the Palestinian territories and neighbouring areas.

sources.title: Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week
Israeli measures tighten grip on Hebron’s Ibrahimi Mosque
Indonesia receives bodies of peacekeepers killed in southern Lebanon

world.headline: Germany Eyes Military Travel Curbs; Estonia Dismisses Russia Rumours
world.body:
Germany is considering requiring men under 45 to obtain military approval before taking extended stays abroad, a move reflecting Berlin's push to shore up defence readiness as European security concerns deepen. The proposal has not yet passed but signals how seriously German planners are treating potential mobilisation scenarios.

Separately, rumours circulated that the Estonian city of Narva - home to a large Russian-speaking population - was seeking to join Russia. Estonian officials dismissed the claims as disinformation. The episode highlights ongoing Russian information operations targeting Baltic states, where Kremlin narratives about protecting Russian-speaking minorities have long served as a geopolitical tool.

sources.title: 'People's Republic' rumors: Does an Estonian city really want to join Russia?
German males under 45 may need military approval for long stays abroad

ai.headline: Claude Code Leak Weaponized, Anthropic Adds Pricing Friction
ai.body:
Hackers are distributing a leaked version of Claude Code bundled with malware, turning the breach into an active threat vector for developers hunting free tools. The timing is awkward for Anthropic, which simultaneously announced that Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra to access OpenClaw support, adding a new billing layer on top of existing subscriptions.

The dual hit - a security crisis and a pricing squeeze - puts Anthropic on the defensive. Karpathy's recently shared LLM Wiki, framed as an 'idea file' for working with language models, is circulating widely at the same moment, a reminder of how much developer mindshare these tools now command. Security researchers warn that malware-laced leaks targeting AI coding tools are a growing pattern, exploiting developer eagerness to access premium features for free.

sources.title: Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
LLM Wiki – example of an "idea file"

ai.headline: AI Fakes and Copyright Trolls Are Targeting Independent Artists
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell has become a case study in how AI-generated fakes and opportunistic copyright trolls can devastate independent artists simultaneously. Fake tracks mimicking Campbell's style flooded streaming platforms while a separate troll filed copyright claims against his actual work, leaving him fighting on two fronts with limited legal recourse.

The story lands alongside a broader cultural anxiety about provenance. The Verge reports growing demand for 'human-made' labels and verification schemes as buyers and audiences struggle to trust creative work. That pressure is real but the infrastructure to support it barely exists. For working musicians without label backing, the current environment offers little protection and no easy fix.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair Faces Industry Assault in Colorado
ai.body:
Tech companies are lobbying hard to gut Colorado's right-to-repair law before it takes full effect, pushing amendments that would hollow out consumer protections while preserving the appearance of compliance. Colorado's law was among the strongest in the country when passed, covering a broad range of devices and requiring manufacturers to supply parts and diagnostics to independent repairers.

The industry playbook is familiar - accept the headline, fight the details. Proposed carve-outs and narrow definitions of 'repair' could render the law largely symbolic. Meanwhile, Germany's eIDAS digital identity rollout drew separate criticism after it emerged the implementation will require users to hold an Apple or Google account, handing two American platforms a chokehold over European digital identity infrastructure.

sources.title: Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law
German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half
ai.body:
An AWS engineer has reported that upgrading to Linux 7.0 roughly halves PostgreSQL throughput in some configurations, a serious regression for one of the most widely deployed database stacks in the world. The root cause points to scheduler and memory subsystem changes in the new kernel, and a clean fix is not straightforward.

The finding matters beyond AWS. Any organization running PostgreSQL on Linux 7.0 could be absorbing a silent performance penalty without knowing it. Kernel maintainers are aware of the report but have not committed to a timeline for resolution. For teams planning infrastructure upgrades, the advice for now is to benchmark carefully before moving production workloads to the new kernel.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy

btc.headline: Drift Protocol Exploit Was Months in the Making
btc.body:
The $280 million Drift Protocol exploit was not opportunistic. Preliminary findings show attackers spent months preparing the attack, suggesting deep familiarity with the protocol's architecture and a level of patience rarely seen in DeFi hacks.

The scale and sophistication put Drift among the largest decentralized exchange exploits on record. Investigators are still working to trace the funds and identify the actors involved. The case raises hard questions about how long vulnerabilities can sit undetected in live protocols handling hundreds of millions in user assets.

sources.title: Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

btc.headline: Bitcoin Sentiment Turns Bearish as Price Stagnates
btc.body:
Bearish chatter around Bitcoin on social media has hit a five-week high, according to data from Santiment. The spike in negative sentiment tracks closely with a period of price stagnation, as traders grow impatient waiting for a decisive move in either direction.

Historically, elevated bearish sentiment at flat price levels has sometimes preceded recoveries, as retail pessimism peaks before institutional buyers step in. Whether that pattern holds this time depends heavily on broader macro conditions, which remain unsettled.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment

btc.headline: Bitcoin's Quantum Threat Is Real. The Fix Is Complicated.
btc.body:
With Bitcoin's market cap sitting at $1.3 trillion, the push to make the network resistant to quantum computing attacks is accelerating. Several initiatives are now underway to develop and eventually deploy post-quantum cryptographic standards before quantum hardware reaches the capability to break existing encryption.

The challenge is not just technical. Upgrading Bitcoin requires broad consensus across a notoriously fractious developer community, and any misstep in implementation could introduce new vulnerabilities. The window to act may be longer than alarmists suggest, but the complexity of the solution means work has to start now.

sources.title: Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leeks with Soft Eggs
food.description: Charred leeks brushed with a sweet-salty miso butter, served over creamy soft-boiled eggs and toasted sesame rice
food.tip: Halve the leeks lengthways and press them cut-side down in a dry pan until deeply caramelised before adding the glaze — patience here is everything

exercise.name: The Sunday Tempo Walk
exercise.description: A brisk outdoor walk structured as 10 min easy, 25 min at a pace where speaking full sentences is just uncomfortable, 10 min easy cool-down — deceptively effective cardiovascular training that won't wreck your rest day

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same thing — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a surprisingly sharp question about whether you are the same person you were ten years ago
learn.why: On a quiet Sunday it's the perfect length of rabbit hole: start with the original paradox, drift into Locke's memory theory of identity, and surface wondering something genuinely unsettling about yourself
